risk_findings.title: [kube-system/namespace-controller] ServiceAccount can get/read Secret values
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "namespace-controller" in namespace "kube-system" can read raw Secret values (tokens, passwords, certificates) via GET.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:kube-system:namespace-controller can read raw Secret values (tokens, passwords, certificates) via GET — bound via: ClusterRole/system:controller:namespace-controller (via system:controller:namespace-controller)
risk_findings.mitigation: Restrict secret get to specific resourceNames. Migrate to external secret stores.

risk_findings.title: [kube-system/namespace-controller] ServiceAccount can list Secrets
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "namespace-controller" in namespace "kube-system" can enumerate all Secret names in accessible namespaces.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:kube-system:namespace-controller can enumerate all Secret names in accessible namespaces — bound via: ClusterRole/system:controller:namespace-controller (via system:controller:namespace-controller)
risk_findings.mitigation: Restrict Secret list to specific resourceNames. Enable audit logging for list events.

risk_findings.title: [kube-system/namespace-controller] Wildcard RBAC grant
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "namespace-controller" in namespace "kube-system" has a wildcard RBAC rule (verbs=[delete deletecollection get list watch], resources=[*]). This grants broad and potentially unintended permissions.
evidence: Rule: verbs=[delete deletecollection get list watch] resources=[*] apiGroups=[*]
risk_findings.mitigation: Replace wildcard grants with specific verb+resource combinations (least-privilege).

risk_findings.title: [kube-system/clusterrole-aggregation-controller] ServiceAccount can patch ClusterRoles — arbitrary permission escalation
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "clusterrole-aggregation-controller" in namespace "kube-system" can patch ClusterRole definitions, adding arbitrary rules to any existing cluster role.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:kube-system:clusterrole-aggregation-controller can patch ClusterRole definitions, adding arbitrary rules to any existing cluster role — bound via: ClusterRole/system:controller:clusterrole-aggregation-controller (via system:controller:clusterrole-aggregation-controller)
risk_findings.mitigation: Treat ClusterRole patch as equivalent to cluster-admin. Remove from non-platform-team accounts.

risk_findings.title: [kube-system/clusterrole-aggregation-controller] ServiceAccount has escalate on ClusterRoles → can create rules beyond current permissions
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "clusterrole-aggregation-controller" in namespace "kube-system" has escalate permission on clusterroles, allowing creation/modification of roles with permissions it does not hold.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:kube-system:clusterrole-aggregation-controller has escalate permission on clusterroles, allowing creation/modification of roles with permissions it does not hold — bound via: ClusterRole/system:controller:clusterrole-aggregation-controller (via system:controller:clusterrole-aggregation-controller)
risk_findings.mitigation: Remove escalate from all non-platform-team accounts. Alert on escalation events in audit logs.

risk_findings.title: [k8scout-test/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount can create ClusterRoleBindings — cluster-wide privilege escalation
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" in namespace "k8scout-test" can create ClusterRoleBindings, enabling binding to cluster-admin or any ClusterRole.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:k8scout-test:cluster-admin-sa can create ClusterRoleBindings, enabling binding to cluster-admin or any ClusterRole — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-test-binding)
risk_findings.mitigation: Remove create/patch on clusterrolebindings from this SA. Restrict to break-glass accounts only.

risk_findings.title: [k8scout-test/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount can create RoleBindings — namespace privilege escalation
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" in namespace "k8scout-test" can create RoleBindings, enabling namespace-scoped privilege escalation.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:k8scout-test:cluster-admin-sa can create RoleBindings, enabling namespace-scoped privilege escalation — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-test-binding)
risk_findings.mitigation: Restrict RoleBinding creation to dedicated CI/CD service accounts with audit alerting.

risk_findings.title: [k8scout-test/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount can impersonate users/SAs — identity takeover
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" in namespace "k8scout-test" has impersonate permission, allowing it to act as any other user or service account.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:k8scout-test:cluster-admin-sa has impersonate permission, allowing it to act as any other user or service account — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-test-binding)
risk_findings.mitigation: The impersonate verb should only be held by aggregated API server accounts. Remove immediately.

risk_findings.title: [k8scout-test/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount can patch ClusterRoles — arbitrary permission escalation
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" in namespace "k8scout-test" can patch ClusterRole definitions, adding arbitrary rules to any existing cluster role.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:k8scout-test:cluster-admin-sa can patch ClusterRole definitions, adding arbitrary rules to any existing cluster role — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-test-binding)
risk_findings.mitigation: Treat ClusterRole patch as equivalent to cluster-admin. Remove from non-platform-team accounts.

risk_findings.title: [k8scout-test/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount can create SA tokens — long-lived credential generation
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" in namespace "k8scout-test" can generate ServiceAccount tokens for any SA, producing long-lived impersonation credentials.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:k8scout-test:cluster-admin-sa can generate ServiceAccount tokens for any SA, producing long-lived impersonation credentials — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-test-binding)
risk_findings.mitigation: Restrict serviceaccounts/token create to operator accounts. Prefer projected volumes.

risk_findings.title: [k8scout-test/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount can get/read Secret values
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" in namespace "k8scout-test" can read raw Secret values (tokens, passwords, certificates) via GET.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:k8scout-test:cluster-admin-sa can read raw Secret values (tokens, passwords, certificates) via GET — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-test-binding)
risk_findings.mitigation: Restrict secret get to specific resourceNames. Migrate to external secret stores.

risk_findings.title: [k8scout-test/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount can list Secrets
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" in namespace "k8scout-test" can enumerate all Secret names in accessible namespaces.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:k8scout-test:cluster-admin-sa can enumerate all Secret names in accessible namespaces — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-test-binding)
risk_findings.mitigation: Restrict Secret list to specific resourceNames. Enable audit logging for list events.

risk_findings.title: [k8scout-test/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount can patch Deployments — workload takeover / SA lateral movement
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" in namespace "k8scout-test" can patch Deployments, injecting containers, changing service accounts, or replacing images.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:k8scout-test:cluster-admin-sa can patch Deployments, injecting containers, changing service accounts, or replacing images — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-test-binding)
risk_findings.mitigation: Limit deployment patch to dedicated CD service accounts. Use Kyverno/OPA for pod spec validation.

risk_findings.title: [k8scout-test/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount can patch DaemonSets — node-level code execution
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" in namespace "k8scout-test" can patch DaemonSets; changes roll out to every node in the cluster simultaneously.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:k8scout-test:cluster-admin-sa can patch DaemonSets; changes roll out to every node in the cluster simultaneously — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-test-binding)
risk_findings.mitigation: Strictly limit DaemonSet modifications. Apply Pod Security Admission baseline/restricted.

risk_findings.title: [k8scout-test/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount can create Pods — potential node escape if PSA is permissive
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" in namespace "k8scout-test" can create Pods; with a permissive PSA profile this enables privileged pod / node escape.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:k8scout-test:cluster-admin-sa can create Pods; with a permissive PSA profile this enables privileged pod / node escape — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-test-binding)
risk_findings.mitigation: Enforce Pod Security Admission at 'restricted' level. Block hostPID/hostNetwork via OPA.

risk_findings.title: [k8scout-test/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount can exec into Pods — direct container shell access
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" in namespace "k8scout-test" can exec into running containers, gaining direct shell access to workload environments.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:k8scout-test:cluster-admin-sa can exec into running containers, gaining direct shell access to workload environments — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-test-binding)
risk_findings.mitigation: Restrict pods/exec to break-glass accounts. Alert on exec events via audit policy.

risk_findings.title: [k8scout-test/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount has escalate on ClusterRoles → can create rules beyond current permissions
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" in namespace "k8scout-test" has escalate permission on clusterroles, allowing creation/modification of roles with permissions it does not hold.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:k8scout-test:cluster-admin-sa has escalate permission on clusterroles, allowing creation/modification of roles with permissions it does not hold — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-test-binding)
risk_findings.mitigation: Remove escalate from all non-platform-team accounts. Alert on escalation events in audit logs.

risk_findings.title: [k8scout-test/cluster-admin-sa] ServiceAccount has bind on ClusterRoles → can create ClusterRoleBindings for any ClusterRole
risk_findings.description: ServiceAccount "cluster-admin-sa" in namespace "k8scout-test" has bind permission on clusterroles, allowing creation of ClusterRoleBindings for cluster-admin or any other ClusterRole.
evidence: system:serviceaccount:k8scout-test:cluster-admin-sa has bind permission on clusterroles, allowing creation of ClusterRoleBindings for cluster-admin or any other ClusterRole — bound via: ClusterRole/cluster-admin (via cluster-admin-test-binding)
risk_findings.mitigation: Remove bind from all non-platform-team accounts. Combined with escalate this grants full cluster-admin.

risk_findings.mitigation: Replace wildcard grants with specific verb+resource combinations (least-privilege).

risk_findings.title: Identity can list Secrets
risk_findings.description: The current identity can list Secrets in 6 namespace(s). Any actor with this token can retrieve plaintext secret values directly via the Kubernetes API.
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Restrict Secret RBAC to specific resourceNames (least-privilege per SA).
• Migrate credentials to an external secret store (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, ESO).
• Enable Kubernetes audit logging for secret access and alert on anomalies.
• Apply NetworkPolicies to limit which pods can reach the API server.

risk_findings.title: Privileged containers detected in cluster
risk_findings.description: 6 privileged container(s) detected. Privileged containers have full access to the host kernel and can be used for container breakout.
evidence: Workload kube-system/calico-node has privileged containers: [upgrade-ipam install-cni mount-bpffs calico-node]
Pod kube-system/calico-node-7lmxh has privileged containers: [upgrade-ipam install-cni mount-bpffs calico-node]
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Replace privileged containers with specific Linux capabilities (CAP_NET_ADMIN etc.) as needed.
• Enforce Pod Security Admission 'restricted' or 'baseline' to prevent privileged containers.
• Audit why each privileged container requires elevated access and minimize.
• Use Falco to detect privileged container activity at runtime.

risk_findings.title: HostPath volumes detected — host filesystem exposure
risk_findings.description: 9 workload(s)/pod(s) mount sensitive hostPath volumes. These grant containers read/write access to host filesystem paths, enabling credential theft or container escape.
evidence: pod:default:vuln-pod mounts "/var/run/docker.sock" [CRITICAL: Docker socket → container escape]
pod:kube-system:kube-scheduler-minikube mounts "/etc/kubernetes/scheduler.conf" [CRITICAL: cluster config → credential theft]
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Replace hostPath mounts with PersistentVolumeClaims.
• If hostPath is required, use readOnly=true and restrict to the minimum path.
• Block hostPath via OPA/Gatekeeper or Kyverno policy.
• Especially dangerous paths: /, /etc, /var/run/docker.sock, /run/containerd, /etc/kubernetes/pki.

risk_findings.title: Pods using host PID/Network namespace — container isolation bypass
risk_findings.description: Workloads are sharing host PID or network namespaces, significantly reducing container isolation and enabling attacks against host processes.
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Remove hostPID and hostNetwork from all workloads that don't require them.
• hostPID allows process inspection/signal of all host processes.
• hostNetwork exposes the host network stack and can bypass NetworkPolicies.
• Enforce via Pod Security Admission 'restricted' policy.

risk_findings.title: Wildcard verb or resource grants detected in RBAC rules
risk_findings.description: 10 RBAC role(s) contain wildcard verb or resource grants. These are difficult to audit and often grant unintended permissions.
evidence: Role /system:controller:generic-garbage-collector has wildcard: verbs=[delete get list patch update watch] resources=[*]
Role /system:controller:horizontal-pod-autoscaler has wildcard: verbs=[get list watch] resources=[*]
Role /system:controller:horizontal-pod-autoscaler has wildcard: verbs=[get list watch] resources=[*]
Role /system:controller:namespace-controller has wildcard: verbs=[delete deletecollection get list watch] resources=[*]
Role /system:controller:resourcequota-controller has wildcard: verbs=[list watch] resources=[*]
Role /system:kube-controller-manager has wildcard: verbs=[list watch] resources=[*]
Role /system:kubelet-api-admin has wildcard: verbs=[*] resources=[nodes/log nodes/metrics nodes/proxy nodes/stats]
Role /system:kubelet-api-admin has wildcard: verbs=[*] resources=[nodes/configz nodes/healthz nodes/pods]
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Replace '*' verbs/resources with explicit lists following least-privilege.
• Audit each ClusterRole/Role with wildcards and replace with minimal permission sets.
• Use 'kubectl auth reconcile' to diff and apply minimal RBAC manifests.

risk_findings.title: Non-system identity bound to cluster-admin ClusterRole
risk_findings.description: Non-system identities are directly bound to the cluster-admin ClusterRole. These identities have unrestricted access to all cluster resources.
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Immediately review and remove non-emergency cluster-admin bindings.
• Use namespace-scoped admin roles instead of cluster-admin wherever possible.
• Implement MPA or GitOps approval workflows for cluster-admin binding changes.
• Enable audit alerting on any cluster-admin role use.

risk_findings.title: Workloads with automountServiceAccountToken not explicitly disabled
risk_findings.description: 8 workload(s) automatically mount a ServiceAccount token. If these workloads don't need API access, the token is unnecessary attack surface.
evidence: Workload k8scout-target/cloud-identity-app does not disable automountServiceAccountToken
Workload k8scout-target/normal-app does not disable automountServiceAccountToken
Workload k8scout-target/privileged-app does not disable automountServiceAccountToken
Workload k8scout-test/webhook-server does not disable automountServiceAccountToken
Workload kube-system/calico-kube-controllers does not disable automountServiceAccountToken
Workload kube-system/coredns does not disable automountServiceAccountToken
Workload kube-system/calico-node does not disable automountServiceAccountToken
Workload kube-system/kube-proxy does not disable automountServiceAccountToken
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Set automountServiceAccountToken: false on all pods/SAs that don't need API access.
• This prevents the SA token from being injected into the pod filesystem, reducing SSRF blast radius.

risk_findings.title: ServiceAccount with cloud IAM role annotation — cross-cloud escalation path
risk_findings.description: 2 ServiceAccount(s) carry cloud IAM role annotations. Any workload running as these SAs can call cloud APIs (S3, IAM, KMS, etc.) using the pod's projected service account token — no secrets required. Compromising these pods enables cross-cloud privilege escalation.
evidence: [AWS] SA k8scout-test/cloud-identity-sa → AWS cloud role "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/k8scout-test-role" (no workload bound — dormant)
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Audit all cloud IAM roles bound to Kubernetes service accounts.
• Apply least-privilege IAM policies to IRSA/Workload-Identity roles.
• Use IAM Conditions (AWS: aws:RequestedRegion, GCP: resource.name) to limit scope.
• Enable CloudTrail/Cloud Audit Logs for actions performed via workload identity.
• Revoke unused IRSA roles immediately; any pod running as this SA can call cloud APIs.

risk_findings.title: Identity can patch MutatingWebhookConfigurations → intercept any pod
risk_findings.description: The current identity can modify MutatingWebhookConfigurations. An attacker can redirect admission requests to a rogue HTTPS endpoint, mutating any new pod spec to inject malicious containers, steal SA tokens, or add environment variables with exfiltration payloads. Attack: patch the webhook to point to attacker.example.com, then wait for pod creation.
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Patching MutatingWebhookConfigurations allows redirecting all admission requests to an attacker-controlled endpoint.
• Restrict patch/create on mutatingwebhookconfigurations to platform-team break-glass accounts only.
• Use OPA/Gatekeeper to require webhook configurations to point to approved service endpoints.
• Alert on any change to MutatingWebhookConfigurations via audit policy.

risk_findings.title: Critical hostPath + exec access → node-level compromise path
risk_findings.description: CRITICAL: The current identity can exec into 2 pod(s) that mount critical host paths (/etc/kubernetes/pki, /var/lib/kubelet). Attack: exec into pod → read kubelet client cert or cluster CA key → forge credentials for any cluster identity.
evidence: pod:kube-system:kube-controller-manager-minikube mounts "/etc/kubernetes/controller-manager.conf" AND identity can exec → exec in → read node credentials
pod:kube-system:kube-scheduler-minikube mounts "/etc/kubernetes/scheduler.conf" AND identity can exec → exec in → read node credentials
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Remove hostPath mounts to critical paths (/etc/kubernetes, /var/lib/kubelet, /etc/kubernetes/pki).
• Restrict pods/exec to break-glass accounts only; use ephemeral containers for debugging.
• Enable Pod Security Admission 'restricted' to block hostPath on production workloads.
• Use Falco to detect exec into pods that have critical hostPath mounts.

risk_findings.title: Identity can use nodes/proxy subresource — direct kubelet API access
risk_findings.description: The current identity can use the nodes/proxy subresource, which tunnels arbitrary HTTP requests directly to the kubelet API. This bypasses RBAC for kubelet endpoints, enabling attackers to read pod logs, execute commands, and access node-level secrets.
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Remove nodes/proxy from all non-system service accounts.
• nodes/proxy allows direct, unauthenticated-from-kubelet-perspective requests that bypass RBAC on the kubelet.
• Enable NodeRestriction admission plugin (default since k8s 1.17) and audit logs on kubelet access.
• Rotate any credential that has held this permission.

risk_findings.title: Identity can patch/update Secrets — credential overwrite vector
risk_findings.description: The current identity can patch or update Secrets in 6 namespace(s). An attacker can overwrite secret data values — replacing credentials with attacker-controlled values or inserting malicious content consumed by workloads.
risk_findings.mitigation:
• patch/update on secrets is as dangerous as read access — an attacker can overwrite any credential.
• Restrict secret mutation to dedicated secret-management service accounts only.
• Enable audit logging for secret patch/update and alert immediately.
• Consider using external secret stores where mutation is controlled by the store's own ACL.

risk_findings.title: Identity can create DaemonSets — cluster-wide code execution
risk_findings.description: The current identity can create DaemonSets. A DaemonSet schedules a pod on every node simultaneously. An attacker can create a DaemonSet with a privileged container or hostPath mount to achieve node-level code execution across the entire cluster.
risk_findings.mitigation:
• DaemonSets schedule pods on every node — creating one is equivalent to cluster-wide code execution.
• Restrict daemonsets create to cluster administrators only.
• Use admission webhooks (OPA/Gatekeeper, Kyverno) to enforce Pod Security Standards on new DaemonSets.
• Audit all DaemonSet creations and alert on any created outside standard CI/CD pipelines.

risk_findings.title: Pods using host IPC namespace — cross-process memory introspection
risk_findings.description: 1 workload(s)/pod(s) share the host IPC namespace. This allows containers to inspect shared memory segments of host processes, potentially exposing sensitive data or enabling inter-process attacks.
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Remove hostIPC from all workloads that do not have a documented requirement.
• hostIPC allows a container to attach to shared memory segments of host processes, enabling memory scraping.
• Enforce Pod Security Admission 'baseline' or 'restricted' to block hostIPC.
• Use Falco to detect container processes accessing host IPC namespaces.

risk_findings.title: Identity can patch ServiceAccounts — workload identity injection
risk_findings.description: The current identity can patch ServiceAccounts in 6 namespace(s). An attacker can inject IRSA/Azure Workload Identity/GKE Workload Identity annotations, granting pods using that SA elevated cloud IAM permissions.
risk_findings.mitigation:
• patch/update on serviceaccounts allows adding IRSA/Workload Identity annotations to any SA.
• An attacker can annotate any SA with a high-privilege cloud IAM role ARN, then pods using that SA gain cloud access.
• Restrict serviceaccount mutation to dedicated identity management accounts.
• Enable audit logging and alert on annotation changes to service accounts.

risk_findings.title: Identity can watch Secrets — continuous credential streaming
risk_findings.description: The current identity can watch Secrets in 6 namespace(s). The watch verb opens a streaming connection that delivers every secret create/update event, providing continuous access to all credential changes.
risk_findings.mitigation:
• watch on secrets streams all secret creation and update events in real time.
• This is equivalent to persistent read access — an attacker gets every secret change automatically.
• Revoke watch permission on secrets unless strictly required for a controller.
• Enable audit logging for secret watch events and alert on unexpected principals.

risk_findings.title: Identity can patch/update Nodes — label manipulation and scheduling attacks
risk_findings.description: The current identity can patch or update Node objects. An attacker can manipulate node labels to influence pod scheduling, remove protective taints (e.g., NoSchedule), or corrupt node status information used by the scheduler and controllers.
risk_findings.mitigation:
• patch/update on nodes allows modifying labels and taints, influencing pod scheduling.
• An attacker can remove NoSchedule taints from sensitive nodes or add labels to steer workloads.
• Restrict node mutation to system:nodes group and dedicated node management controllers.
• Monitor for unexpected node label or taint changes via audit logging.

risk_findings.title: Identity can patch/update StatefulSets — persistent volume access + SA lateral movement
risk_findings.description: The current identity can patch or update StatefulSets. An attacker can modify the pod template to inject a malicious container or change the service account, gaining access to persistent volumes and the permissions of the patched StatefulSet's SA.
risk_findings.mitigation:
• patch/update on statefulsets enables injecting new containers, changing the service account, or modifying volume mounts.
• StatefulSets use PersistentVolumes — a patched container can access all existing persistent data.
• Restrict statefulset mutation to dedicated deployment accounts.
• Use admission webhooks to block SA changes in StatefulSet patches.

risk_findings.title: Mutating webhook with FailurePolicy=Ignore — security controls may be bypassed on outage
risk_findings.description: 1 mutating webhook(s) use FailurePolicy=Ignore. If the webhook endpoint is unavailable (crash, network partition, cert expiry), the admission controller is silently bypassed — allowing policy violations, image pulls without scanning, or privilege escalation to pass undetected.
risk_findings.mitigation:
• Set FailurePolicy=Fail on all security-critical webhooks (OPA, Kyverno, image admission).
• Accept that Fail policy means webhook outages block deployments — plan for availability accordingly.
• Use redundant webhook replicas (minReplicas≥2, PodDisruptionBudget) to reduce outage risk.
• Alert when webhook endpoints become unavailable (Prometheus probe or synthetic check).
